summary: I went to Melrose High School back home on the farm. I wasn’t a distinguished scholar, and in my senior year, I made a deal with the principal to leave early with the condition that if I passed the GED and Basic or AIT, they would issue me a diploma. My class graduated in May 1965, but I technically graduated in July 1965 after passing those requirements. Before entering the military, I was working part-time as a store clerk in the local grocery store in Melrose.

I started my journey from La Crosse, Wisconsin when I entered military service on February 10th, 1965. From there, I traveled to the Twin Cities before heading to Fort Leonard Wood for basic training. La Crosse was the last place I was before stepping into the Army world, coming from my farm and small-town life.

After leaving La Crosse, I went to the Twin Cities as part of my transition into the Army. It was a stopover before heading to Fort Leonard Wood, Missouri, for basic training and AIT in early 1965. The Twin Cities was just a waypoint on my way from home to the Army life.

I did my basic training and Advanced Individual Training at Fort Leonard Wood, Missouri, starting in February 1965. It was one of those Army bases located in the worst spot in the state, but as a farm kid, the physical part wasn’t too hard for me. The training was tough mentally, breaking you down and building you back up to work as a team and learn basic infantry skills.

After finishing AIT, I went to Fort Benning, Georgia, for Jump School in July 1965. It was the peak of the heat in Georgia, and the three weeks of jump training was exactly what I wanted when I enlisted. Fort Benning was where I learned to jump out of airplanes, which was my main goal in joining the Army.

Georgia was where Fort Benning is located, and I was there in July 1965 for Jump School. It was really hot during that time, but I was determined to complete the airborne training. Georgia was my introduction to the southern heat and the intense physical challenge of becoming a paratrooper.

I attended the United States Army Airborne School at Fort Benning in July 1965. I had asked specifically to go to Jump School when I enlisted because I wanted to jump out of airplanes. The school was three weeks long, and I was the only one from my Basic and AIT group who actually made it through and earned my wings.

After Jump School, I was assigned to the 1st Cavalry Division, which had just been renamed from the 11th Air Assault Division. This was in mid-1965, and the 1st Brigade of the division was preparing to deploy to the Republic of Vietnam. I left for Vietnam in August 1965 as part of this division, which was a big moment for me coming from Wisconsin to a combat zone.

The 11th Air Assault Division was the original name of the unit I was assigned to before it was renamed the 1st Cavalry Division. This change happened around 1965 as the division prepared for deployment to Vietnam. I was part of the transition and went with the unit as it became the 1st Cav Division.

I was deployed to the Republic of Vietnam in August 1965 with the 1st Cavalry Division. We traveled by ship on the USS Geiger from Savannah, Georgia, taking 32 days and enduring a hurricane along the way. It was my first time outside Wisconsin, and arriving in Vietnam was a huge change from my farm life back home.

The USS Geiger was the ship that transported me and about three thousand soldiers of the 1st Brigade from Savannah, Georgia, to Vietnam in August 1965. The trip took 32 days and included surviving a hurricane and a twelve-hour stop in Hawaii. It was a long and challenging journey for a farm kid like me going into combat for the first time.

During our voyage to Vietnam in 1965 aboard the USS Geiger, we stopped in Hawaii for about twelve hours. That was my first real exposure to a place outside the Midwest, and it was a brief but memorable stop on the way to the war zone. Coming from Wisconsin, Hawaii was a big change and part of the long journey to Vietnam.

While I didn’t serve in World War II, I learned about it during my training and heard stories about battles like the Battle of the Bulge. The Army taught us that even cooks and clerks could end up in infantry roles if the camp was attacked, just like in World War II. It gave me perspective on the seriousness of combat and the importance of basic infantry skills.

The Battle of the Bulge was mentioned during my basic training as an example of how even non-infantry soldiers had to be prepared to fight if their camp was attacked. It was part of the Army’s way of teaching us that everyone needed to know basic combat skills. Although I didn’t experience it, the battle was a reminder of the harsh realities of war.

During basic training, I learned to use various infantry weapons, including the M79 grenade launcher. It was part of the standard infantry training where we practiced throwing grenades and shooting small arms. The M79 was one of the tools we trained with to prepare for combat situations.

After returning from Vietnam the first time, I was assigned to the 6th Special Forces Group at Fort Bragg. It was interesting because a guy I knew from Jump School was just heading to Vietnam while I was back with Special Forces. The 6th Special Forces was a different kind of unit, and being at Fort Bragg was a new chapter after my combat deployment.

Fort Bragg was where I was stationed with the 6th Special Forces Group after my first tour in Vietnam. It was a major Army base and a center for Special Forces training and operations. Being at Fort Bragg was a shift from my earlier infantry and airborne experiences, and it connected me with a different community within the Army.
